metadata.title: The Super Bowl ad you'll never see

content.description:
The Super Bowl ad you’ll never see is a creative spoof that simulates a high-profile commercial conceived to air during the Super Bowl.

The film is built around a fictional yet deliberately realistic scenario: during the shoot, the set is raided by ICE. Actors are detained. Production is abruptly halted. The commercial is pulled before it ever reaches broadcast.

By staging an ICE raid within the familiar language and aesthetics of a Super Bowl commercial, the project uses parody to draw attention to a broader and very real social context. What initially appears to be a classic snack ad gradually turns into a commentary on what is happening in the United States today, using advertising itself as the narrative device.

The film was created entirely using AI and represents the first project by doope, a new house of production specializing in commercials developed through artificial intelligence. This production approach treats AI not merely as a technical tool, but as a creative language—one capable of replicating the codes of traditional advertising and then challenging them from within.

Designed to feel like an ad that “almost made it to air,” The Super Bowl ad you’ll never see blurs the line between brand communication and cultural reflection, leveraging the mythology of the Super Bowl to spark awareness and conversation.

ai_enrichment.concept_summary: An AI-created spoof Super Bowl commercial that depicts its own production being raided by ICE, transforming from a typical snack ad into a powerful social commentary on current events in the United States. It uses parody to draw attention to real social contexts by portraying an ad that was 'pulled' before broadcast.
ai_enrichment.target_audience: Socially conscious individuals and those engaged with current events and social justice in the United States, particularly regarding immigration policies, as well as creative and advertising industry professionals interested in AI's innovative applications.
ai_enrichment.execution_tactics: A hero short film, distributed digitally across platforms like YouTube, Vimeo, and social media, designed to go viral and spark discussion, potentially alongside submissions to film festivals or industry awards given its AI creation aspect.
ai_enrichment.objective: To raise awareness and provoke critical conversation about pressing social issues in the United States, specifically immigration and the impact of ICE raids, by leveraging a familiar yet subverted advertising format.

content.description:
Intuit TurboTax returns to the Super Bowl for the 13th year with a comedic spot starring Oscar winner Adrien Brody. Created by R/GA and directed by Craig Gillespie, the film opens with Brody delivering an intense, rain-soaked monologue, only to be interrupted when the director calls cut. Cast as “The Expert”, he is told TurboTax experts take the pain out of taxes.

Unsettled by being asked to play happy rather than brooding, Brody spirals into a self-aware crisis about his career. The ad follows a teaser campaign introducing him as “The Expert”, supported by wild postings across New York City and Los Angeles to build anticipation ahead of the Big Game.

ai_enrichment.concept_summary: TurboTax leverages Oscar winner Adrien Brody playing 'The Expert' in a comedic Super Bowl spot, contrasting his dramatic portrayal of tax pain with the easy solution provided by TurboTax experts, leading to Brody's humorous self-aware career crisis.
ai_enrichment.target_audience: General taxpayers, particularly those who find tax preparation confusing, stressful, or time-consuming, and are seeking simpler, expert-assisted solutions.
ai_enrichment.execution_tactics: Primary Super Bowl film ad, supported by a pre-launch teaser campaign featuring wild postings across New York City and Los Angeles, and subsequent widespread film distribution.
ai_enrichment.objective: Increase brand awareness for TurboTax and reinforce its value proposition that its experts simplify and remove the pain from tax preparation.

content.description:
Toyota's "Department of Human Technology" campaign aimed to shift brand perceptions among tech-forward Asian Americans who may respect the brand's reliability but do not yet view it as a visionary tech leader. Rooted in a "Humanity First" philosophy, the campaign highlights that true innovation is not just about technology for its own sake, but about amplifying human capabilities and making everyday experiences better.

The creative work centered on the fictional Toyota Department of Human Technology Toyota Department of Human Technology, a cinematic and interesting lab led by our fake Chief Innovation Officer played by Randall Park.  Through deadpan humor and premium visuals, the campaign addressed real pain points through relatable storytelling.

In our Outlast, we demonstrated how our durability evolved from our trust Tacomas to our bZ4x. In Anxiety, we explored concept breakthroughs designed to solve range stress and charging concerns. Through our campaign, we proved that true innovation isn’t always about the loudest features but about making the electric future serve the people.

 

Advertising Agency: Intertrend CommunicationsProduction Company: Lawrence Fells ProductionsPR Agency: Meehan Business Advisers

ai_enrichment.concept_summary: The campaign uses a fictional 'Department of Human Technology' led by Randall Park, employing deadpan humor and premium visuals, to demonstrate Toyota's 'Humanity First' philosophy where technology enhances human capabilities and daily life.
ai_enrichment.target_audience: Tech-forward Asian Americans who respect Toyota's reliability but do not yet perceive it as a visionary tech leader.
ai_enrichment.execution_tactics: A hero film (or series of films) featuring the fictional lab and Chief Innovation Officer played by Randall Park, distributed via digital video channels and potentially television.
ai_enrichment.objective: To shift brand perception of Toyota among tech-forward Asian Americans, repositioning it as a visionary tech leader beyond its established reliability.

content.description:
Batiste, the world’s #1 dry shampoo brand, today announced the launch of Pass the Batiste, a new global brand platform and its most expansive campaign to date.

The integrated campaign is rooted in a powerful strategic insight: while many women believe dry shampoo is solely for cleaning oily hair between washes, 64%1 of the time it is used for other benefits such as bringing volume, texture, or fragrance to hair.

Pass the Batiste taps into this knowledge, challenging existing perceptions of dry shampoo and sharing consumers’ favourite ways to use the product.At the heart of the campaign is a 30-second hero film, directed by McKenzie Thompson, which showcases Batiste's versatile benefits with vibrant energy. It features a variety of hair types and scenarios, showcasing how the dry shampoo brand can instantly refresh hair for volume, texture, or simply to reduce oil, anytime and anywhere.

The static campaign elements were photographed by Hanina - known for her bold, colourful, and empowering style - and feature bright stills of Batiste being joyfully shared, capturing moments of real-life friendship and connection.

The campaign launches on 9th February in the UK, before rolling out to various countries including France and Germany, Canada, Australia and beyond throughout 2026.

ai_enrichment.concept_summary: The 'Pass the Batiste' campaign aims to reframe dry shampoo's purpose, moving beyond its perception as solely for oily hair to highlight its versatile benefits for adding volume, texture, and fragrance across diverse hair types.
ai_enrichment.target_audience: Women who currently use dry shampoo primarily for cleaning oily hair, as well as potential new users who could benefit from its styling properties, aged broadly across adult demographics who care about hair appearance.
ai_enrichment.execution_tactics: Integrated campaign featuring a central 30-second hero film, showcasing diverse hair types and usage scenarios.
ai_enrichment.objective: To challenge existing perceptions of dry shampoo, educate consumers on its multi-functional benefits beyond cleaning, and increase its relevance for styling, texture, and fragrance.

content.description:
Life’s most beautiful moments aren’t found far away— they’re in the everyday time we share with family.

Even when life takes us far from home, the memories we’ve created and the encouragement we give one another continue to support us as we move forward. As long as our hearts stay connected, home is never truly far.

In this new year, 𝑲𝒏𝒊𝒇𝒆 Cooking Oil will continue to be there for your family moments, bringing 𝐌𝐨𝐫𝐞 𝐀𝐫𝐨𝐦𝐚, 𝐌𝐨𝐫𝐞 𝐓𝐚𝐬𝐭𝐞 to every meal, and fuelling happiness for the whole family.

content.description:
A slick, Severance-style journey between Home and Home-Home — parallel worlds that mirror and distort each other through sharp transitions and shifting characters. We move between young adults navigating their cool city life and their warm family homes, alive with Festive celebrations. Across both realities, one constant remains: KFC.

content.description:
Australia is facing a mounting e-waste problem. Our e-waste production is already triple the global average and projections indicate that by 2030, we'll be producing 657,000 tonnes of e-waste every year1. That's roughly the weight of 12 Sydney Harbour Bridges2 or 19,000 Melbourne trams3.

While those numbers are vast, the problem is deeply personal. Australians hold onto an average of three unused devices each - around 65 million devices sitting idle in homes across the country4.

So, to inspire the nation to give their old devices a second life, we've partnered with ARIA-award winning electronic duo The Avalanches, globally renowned for their sample-driven sound, repurposing fragments of vintage records, tapes and found audio into innovative, intricately layered compositions that have defined their distinctive place in contemporary electronic music and pop culture.

The Avalanches have helped us create a brand-new, fully functioning synthesiser, made up of over 80% repurposed components from e-waste, such as mobile phones, gaming consoles and old electronic cables.

"We wanted to show that e-waste can be repurposed into something that people can relate to universally. Through music, we have tried to demonstrate the amazing link between sustainability and creativity."

ai_enrichment.concept_summary: Telstra partnered with ARIA-award winning electronic duo The Avalanches to inspire Australians to give their old electronic devices a 'second life' by repurposing them into unique sound-based creations, mirroring the band's sampling artistry.
ai_enrichment.target_audience: Australians who own unused electronic devices, across various demographics, with a general concern for environmental issues or an interest in creative reuse.
ai_enrichment.execution_tactics: Partnership with The Avalanches to create content or an activation around repurposing e-waste into sounds, leveraging the band's reputation for sample-driven music. Likely integrated across various channels given the 'Integrated' medium.
ai_enrichment.objective: To inspire and educate Australians about the e-waste problem and encourage the nation to give their old devices a 'second life' through creative repurposing or responsible recycling.

content.description:
Healthcare doesn’t need louder claims or dramatic promises.It needs clarity, reassurance, and a sense of calm credibility that patients can trust.

For NBCC, our challenge was to translate complex medical excellence into communication that feels human and approachable.Especially on social media, where attention is short and emotional clarity matters more than technical depth.

We simplified the language and removed visual noise to create space for trust.Each post was designed to feel composed, respectful, and emotionally intelligent.

Rather than promoting services, we focused on shaping perception and reinforcing confidence.Trust became the message, and care became the tone.

The result is a digital presence that reflects NBCC’s true value through consistency, clarity, and quiet authority.

ai_enrichment.concept_summary: The core creative idea is to translate complex medical excellence into human and approachable communication, emphasizing clarity, reassurance, and calm credibility to build patient trust, especially on social media.
ai_enrichment.target_audience: Patients and the general public seeking healthcare services, particularly those who engage with healthcare information on social media and prioritize trust, emotional reassurance, and clarity over technical depth.
ai_enrichment.execution_tactics: Primarily digital presence, focusing on social media posts with simplified language and reduced visual noise. Each post was designed to be composed, respectful, and emotionally intelligent to convey trust and care.
ai_enrichment.objective: To shape perception and reinforce confidence in NBCC by establishing trust and reflecting its true value through consistent, clear, and authoritative digital communication.

content.description:
Brand strips back to simplicity in new campaign by Broken Heart Love Affair

After more than five decades in the category, In The Raw® is stripping away the excess and letting the brand speak for itself.

The legacy sweetener brand is launching a full-scale masterbrand refresh, which represents a strategic reset that unifies the brand’s platform, portfolio architecture, and creative system under a single point of view. The update spans every major touchpoint, from refreshed packaging and visual identity to digital experiences and an integrated media rollout. Leading the launch is a new video campaign that introduces the refreshed brand to the market, cutting through category noise to refocus In The Raw on simplicity, trust, and honest sweetness. No matter the sweetener, In The Raw.

“This wasn’t just a new campaign; it was an opportunity to reset how the brand shows up everywhere,” said David Goldfarb, COO of Cumberland Packing Corp. “We wanted to build a stronger, more cohesive system that honors our heritage while enabling the brand to keep evolving and growing into the future.”

The launch is led by a comedic hero film that deliberately satirizes the excess that comes from taking the idea of “In The Raw” far too literally. Developed with creative agency Broken Heart Love Affair and directed by Aaron Ruell of Holiday Films, the spot opens with an overconfident, spectacle-driven pitch that escalates into visual absurdity — a tongue-in-cheek commentary on how far brands will go to stand out.

ai_enrichment.concept_summary: The campaign features In The Raw stripping back to its core identity, emphasizing simplicity, trust, and honest sweetness through a comprehensive masterbrand refresh.
ai_enrichment.target_audience: Existing and potential consumers of sweeteners who value simplicity, authenticity, and natural taste.
ai_enrichment.execution_tactics: Integrated media rollout, new video campaign (film), refreshed packaging and visual identity, and updated digital experiences.
ai_enrichment.objective: To reintroduce and unify the In The Raw masterbrand under a single platform, enhancing brand perception and consistency by focusing on simplicity, trust, and honest sweetness.

TK Maxx has unveiled its new creative campaign, Every day can be iconic with a TK Maxx deal, championing the brands-for-less retailer’s belief that style and value should go hand-in-hand, and that everyday styling can be a little more luxurious, a little more designer, and a lot more iconic.

At the heart of the campaign from Wieden+Kennedy London is a new 40-second film directed by award-winning filmmaker Andreas Nilsson, known for his best-in-class, larger-than-life storytelling. Its unexpected hero is an inquisitive cat whose accidental fashion moment sends shockwaves through the style world.

The film opens with the cat rummaging in an iconic red TK Maxx bag and emerging triumphantly with a red loafer perched on her head like a fabulous hat. Her owner snaps a picture and posts it with the caption ‘Cat’s got style’. It instantly goes viral.

 

Yes, we're really doing it, It Snowed Anyway, Re-Edit

content.description:
Wasabi, the popular Japanese and East Asian-inspired food brand, has launched a bold new national on-pack campaign, Lucky Dinners, by All Things, RCP and Unfinished. The campaign celebrates the simple joy of feeling lucky at mealtimes, drawing inspiration from Lunar New Year traditions where the number eight symbolises good fortune across many East Asian cultures.

At the heart of the campaign is a new on-pack promotion across Wasabi’s Meal for One and Feast for One grocery range, giving shoppers the chance to win £8, £88 or £888. The promotion is live now across UK supermarkets including Tesco, Sainsbury’s, Co-op and Morrisons, with full terms and conditions available at wasabiwin.co.uk.

To bring the spirit of Lucky Dinners to life beyond the supermarket aisle, All Things and RCP developed a one-off Lunar New Year gifting moment in Camden Market where the brand’s story first began. Fronted by creator Max Balegde, one of the UK’s most recognisable creators known for spontaneous gifting, the activation saw members of the public surprised with festive red envelopes as a celebratory gesture, capturing the joy of gifting and connection that sits at the heart of the campaign.

Set against the backdrop of a striking Wasabi “Wonder Wall” installation, the gifting moment tapped into the cultural symbolism of Lunar New Year and the universal thrill of an unexpected treat, creating content that feels warm, human and rooted in real life.

Bringing Balegde to Camden is a defining full-circle moment for Wasabi which started as a fresh food market stall in Camden more than 20 years ago, before opening its first official London shop in 2003. That early stall selling sushi and bento food in Camden was the seed for what grew into the UK-wide sushi and hot bento meal brand, whose food is enjoyed at home and at work today.

ai_enrichment.concept_summary: A national on-pack campaign for Wasabi's grocery range, 'Lucky Dinners' draws inspiration from Lunar New Year traditions and the lucky number eight, offering consumers a chance to win cash prizes at mealtimes.
ai_enrichment.target_audience: UK grocery shoppers purchasing Wasabi's 'Meal for One' and 'Feast for One' convenience meal ranges, particularly those looking for an element of fun and luck with their food, and possibly those with an affinity for East Asian cultural themes.
ai_enrichment.execution_tactics: National on-pack promotion across Wasabi’s Meal for One and Feast for One grocery range in major UK supermarkets (Tesco, Sainsbury’s, Co-op, Morrisons), supported by a dedicated campaign website (wasabiwin.co.uk) for terms and conditions.
ai_enrichment.objective: To boost sales and drive engagement for Wasabi's grocery products by providing an exciting chance to win cash prizes, leveraging cultural themes of good fortune.

content.description:
In a world where brands chase visibility, growth often starts with something far more powerful — curiosity.

This film for Deepsense explores how curiosity drives transformation. It showcases the journey of brands that move beyond conventional thinking and embrace exploration, data, technology, and human insight to unlock real growth. Instead of presenting growth as a straight line, the video portrays it as a continuous process of questioning, learning, adapting, and evolving.

Through a visually engaging narrative, the film positions Deepsense as a growth partner that doesn’t just execute marketing — it helps brands discover opportunities they didn’t know existed. The story highlights how curiosity fuels innovation, how insights lead to smarter decisions, and how consistent exploration builds sustainable brand success.

At its core, the film reinforces Deepsense’s philosophy: growth doesn’t come from doing more of the same — it comes from asking better questions.

ai_enrichment.concept_summary: The campaign film positions Deepsense as a growth partner, illustrating how curiosity, driven by data and human insight, fuels innovation and unlocks opportunities for brands beyond conventional thinking.
ai_enrichment.target_audience: Business leaders and decision-makers within brands who are seeking innovative growth strategies and are open to leveraging data, technology, and human insight.
ai_enrichment.execution_tactics: A visually engaging brand film or video narrative.
ai_enrichment.objective: To establish and reinforce Deepsense's position as an innovative growth partner that helps brands unlock opportunities through curiosity and strategic insight.

content.description:
This month, Philadelphia Cream Cheese debuts Really Philly Good, a new brand platform designed to reintroduce the century-old brand as an everyday kitchen staple. Rooted in Philadelphia’s dairy heritage, the platform introduces a new brand character, Phillyboy: a personification of the brand’s journey from farm to kitchens across America.

Developed by Johannes Leonardo, the platform launches with a 360 campaign led by a series of :15, :30, and :60 spots, “The Adventures of the Phillyboy.” With 19 total spots, the films center on four approachable recipes—scrambled eggs, pesto pasta, chili dip and the classic bagel—and feature Phillyboy arriving just in time to elevate everyday meals, showing how a simple touch of Philadelphia can make any dish Really Philly Good.

WHY IT MATTERS: Built on 150+ years of dairy leadership, Philadelphia is redefining its role in culture by introducing a lovable new brand character and showing off its range beyond the expected use cases. Really Philly Good represents a strategic shift for Philly spotlighting the brand as more than just a spread, but a kitchen staple poised to effortlessly enhance a whole host of foods, from pasta and potatoes, to soups, dips and more.HOW IT COMES TO LIFE:Marking the brand’s first creative refresh since 2021 and largest marketing investment to date, the 360 paid media campaign spans TV, streaming/CTV, YouTube, social, audio and creator partnerships.Premium placements with “The Bachelorette” and The New York Times, alongside social and influencer content across TikTok, Meta and Pinterest anchor the platform in both cultural moments and trusted content environments. The plan is designed to drive reach and frequency with key consumers, reinforcing new habits and use cases with a new generation of everyday cooks.In partnership with recipe hub and culinary magazine, Allrecipes, the brand is creating a personalized, AI-powered meal planning tool to help consumers discover new ways to make their meal Really Philly Good. The tool will be accessible on Allrecipes.com.TIME-STRAPPED FLAVOR-SEEKERS: Really Philly Good positions Philadelphia as an easy, confidence-building ingredient that delivers creaminess and flavor without added effort—especially since Philadelphia is a staple in over 131M+ U.S. households.MEET PHILLYBOY: As a brand that sells nearly 413,000 bricks and 250,000 tubs of original cream cheese each day, Philadelphia is the quiet ingredient that has become ubiquitous in kitchens over its 150 year history. Phillyboy brings that legacy to life, embodying the brand’s dairy heritage with a schmear of humanity while empowering home cooks of all skill levels. Introduced with an Origin Story spot, he appears wherever people need him, becoming a familiar face in culture and guiding new generations to take their meals from good to Really Philly Good.

content.description:

content.description:
The Driver and Vehicle Licensing Agency (DVLA) is tackling vehicle tax evasion with a new campaign created by AMV BBDO. The launch marks the agency’s first work for the DVLA.The humorous campaign introduces a new character inspired by one of the DVLA’s most recognisable enforcement tools: the wheel clamp. Affectionately known as “Clampy”, the character stars in two 20-second films that speak directly to vehicle owners who have failed to tax their vehicles.In the first film, Clampy bemoans John for attempting to dodge taxing his car. Chancing it leaves Clampy stuck to John’s vehicle and in the firing line of a passing dog that cocks its leg. Clampy’s troubles don’t stop there. In the second film, he misses his son’s birthday party after absent-minded Leanne forgets to tax her vehicle.Both films end with the car owner coming into view and sighing with frustration when they spot the clamp, playfully dramatising the disruptive reality of forgetting or dodging vehicle tax.Directed by Florence Poppy Deary through Biscuit, the campaign runs across social media including Meta and Reddit, YouTube and other online video platforms, out-of-home, and audio until 31 March.The campaign premiered on audio and out-of-home on 26 January, followed by social and online video on 10 February.Daniel Wood, Head of External Communications at DVLA, said: “This campaign brings a key enforcement route, clamping, to life in a relatable way. Most motorists tax on time, but when they don’t, the consequences can be significant, including having their vehicle clamped. The campaign highlights how quick and easy it is to tax your vehicle online - and paying by Direct Debit can help motorists stay on top of renewals and avoid meeting Clampy.”Ben Edwards, Executive Creative Director, AMV BBDO: “We’re hugely proud that Clampy is our first campaign for the DVLA. Every person he’s met along the creative gauntlet has added something to him, until he became this funny, strange, slightly sad character we all feel incredibly fond of. Hopefully, everyone who sees him out and about feels the same as us and that he makes people think twice about risking not taxing their vehicle, and acts as a gentle reminder for those likely to forget.”

content.description:
In honor of National Caregiver’s Day (February 20), The Stroke Foundation and Remedy Edge (formerly Patients & Purpose and DDB Health) announced the premiere of  SYNCED – a 9-minute short film that spotlights the isolation and emotional strain felt by stroke survivors and their caregivers. In the film, an experimental bracelet allows a stroke survivor to share their thoughts and feelings. According to the Stroke Foundation, stroke affects nearly 800,000 Americans annually, and an estimated 53 million people provide unpaid care to loved ones.The film follows a daughter in her late twenties who has become the unplanned primary caregiver to her mother. As the daughter navigates the frustration and isolation of caregiving, technology becomes the critical bridge that helps them relate to each other and communicate.SYNCED was deliberately crafted to feel like a lost film from the 1980s, shot in the era’s signature 4:3 aspect ratio and brought to life with period-accurate music and props. The experience is retro, until viewers notice the “Synced” bracelets. These unique idealistic devices allow a stroke survivor with limited speech to share her thoughts, feelings and memories directly into her daughter’s mind.  At its heart, SYNCED is a love story about what we lose when a stroke changes someone we love—and what it takes to find each other again.The film showcases unspoken love a daughter has for her mother as she cares for her. Its deep focus is also on the appreciation a parent has for a child caring for them, even if the parent cannot say it herself. Director Ilaria Serantoni said, “The aesthetic of this film—the repetition, the texture, the silence—that's what caregiving can feel like. We wanted to honor that truth visually."The film arrives at a critical time. “As the caregiving crisis grows in America, the system is still not built to support caregivers and stroke is one of the clearest examples,” said Tyler Maxson, Group Creative Director, Remedy Edge. “SYNCED is about the quiet seismic shift that happens inside a relationship after a life-changing health incident. The technology is fictional, but the emotional barriers caregivers and stroke survivors and their caregivers face are not.”By grounding the story in empathy, the film hopes to spark conversations about access, equity and the support caregivers need.“This film speaks to something timeless and unmistakable: a daughter’s love for her mother and the extraordinary people who show up every day for someone else,” said Florencia Rago, Executive Director of The Stroke Foundation. “As someone who has supported my mother through stroke recovery, creating this film was deeply personal for me.”Featuring standout performances from Fran Greene and Glory Gonzalez, SYNCED reminds us that love exists in frequencies that transcend language. You just have to know how to tune in.Visit SyncedFilm.com to watch and share the film and to learn how you can help support caregivers in need via a donation to The Stroke Foundation.

 

Dina Peck, Chief Creative Officer, The Purpose Group (now part of Remedy Edge)Tom Galati, Group Creative DirectorTyler Maxon, Group Creative DirectorIlyssa Berger Wilson, Group Creative DirectorNate Platzner, Senior Video EditorJen Cohen, Group Copy SupervisorDavid Gonzalez, Art Supervisor

ai_enrichment.concept_summary: The Stroke Foundation launched 'SYNCED,' a 9-minute short film featuring an experimental bracelet that allows a stroke survivor to share feelings with her caregiver daughter, highlighting their emotional strain and isolation.
ai_enrichment.target_audience: Unpaid family caregivers of stroke survivors, stroke survivors themselves, and the general public interested in health and caregiving challenges.
ai_enrichment.execution_tactics: Premiere and distribution of a 9-minute short film titled 'SYNCED' in recognition of National Caregiver's Day.
ai_enrichment.objective: To raise awareness about the profound emotional strain and isolation experienced by stroke survivors and their unpaid caregivers, especially around National Caregiver's Day.

content.description:
Five Nights at Freddy’s 2 | Experiential Activation

To support the release of Five Nights at Freddy’s 2, we developed an experiential activation inspired by the film’s iconic pizzeria, designed to live in two complementary formats and scales.

The first activation took place on December 3rd during the avant premiere at Cinépolis Houssay. A reduced version of the concept recreated a pizzeria-themed environment paired with a photo opportunity booth, encouraging guests to generate content and take home a tangible memory of the event. Access was organized through branded wristbands or printed tickets, and each attendee received vouchers to redeem a combo at the candy bar, seamlessly integrating the brand experience into the cinema journey. Approximately 200 guests attended the premiere.

In parallel, a large-scale activation was launched on November 3rd at Plaza Oeste Shopping Mall. This expanded version featured a fully themed pizzeria environment and a photo opp integrated into the spatial flow of the mall, extending the experience to the general public and maximizing visibility, interaction, and organic content creation.

Both executions worked together as a single creative system, adapting the same concept to different contexts and audiences. The project demonstrates the versatility of experiential design to connect with fans through playful, immersive, and visually driven environments.

ai_enrichment.concept_summary: An experiential activation recreating the iconic pizzeria from Five Nights at Freddy's 2, featuring a themed environment and a photo opportunity booth designed to generate content.
ai_enrichment.target_audience: Attendees of the Five Nights at Freddy’s 2 avant premiere, specifically fans of the film franchise.
ai_enrichment.execution_tactics: Experiential activation at a film premiere (Cinépolis Houssay), a pizzeria-themed environment, a photo opportunity booth, branded wristbands/tickets, and candy bar vouchers.
ai_enrichment.objective: To support the release of Five Nights at Freddy’s 2 by encouraging guest engagement, content generation, and integrating the brand experience into the cinema journey.

content.description:
Ortega, the number one selling brand of taco sauces and a household name, is launching a major rebranding effort intended to capture its fair share of the fast-growing international grocery food stores market. The multidisciplinary effort, developed in partnership with its new agency partner, Schafer Condon Carter, includes a new brand identity, new packaging design, product innovations, retail activations and a new advertising platform and spokescharacter.  National advertising rolls out this week, reflecting a new brand platform, “Now it’s a Fiesta,” and featuring a new brand spokescharacter, Brotega, to help humanize the brand and bust out amidst a sea of sameness. Preceding the launch of advertising is a new brand identity and package design which is already on-shelf and which captures the energetic and expressive face of the brand. And everything is backed by an innovative media plan which will ensure that the brand shows up in culture where its targeted audience of Gen Z and Millennials consume their information. In addition to the launch of new advertising, the brand will also field an on-site activation with Celebrity Chef and brand partner Kelsey Murphy, winner of Fox’s MasterChef season 11 and Legends, at the 2026 Players Tailgate in Santa Clara, California, concurrent with this year’s Big Game.

content.description:
Hakata’s identity is anchored in communal rituals, one of the most notable being "Hakata Te Ippon." This traditional rhythmic clapping signifies a harmonious conclusion, gratitude, and a prayer for prosperity. The ritual follows a specific, sacred cadence:

"I-yo!" (Ready) — [Clap, clap]"Ma-hitotsu!" (One more) — [Clap, clap]"Ioute Sando!" (Celebrate three times) — [Three final claps]

This ritual connects people, sealing every important occasion with a spirit of unity. The campaign was launched during the New Year season, Japan’s most significant period for gift-giving and celebratory rituals, which also marks a major peak for international tourism.

As a venerable confectioner established in 1905, Ishimura Manseido is one of the most iconic, long-standing brands in Hakata (the heart of Fukuoka). To mark its 120th anniversary at this pivotal moment, the brand evolved from a "Confectioner" into a "Cultural Storyteller," transforming intangible culture into a tangible sweet: "Ioute Sand." The product name is a linguistic pun on the ritual’s final chant, "Ioute Sando!", phonetically mirroring "Sandwich Cookie."

To satisfy the modern traveler’s desire for immersion, retail staff acted as cultural ambassadors, performing the ritual live during this festive peak and inviting visitors to experience the tradition firsthand. By integrating a "Confectionery Code" handcrafted by confectioners from real ingredients, the brand transformed a simple cookie into a "Tangible Cultural Gateway." This allows tourists to not only taste a sweet but to literally "grasp" and perform Hakata’s soul, making ancient celebratory rituals accessible and shareable for the world.

ai_enrichment.concept_summary: Ishimura Manseido integrates the traditional Hakata 'Te Ippon' ritual into an experiential campaign, connecting people through a shared moment of gratitude and celebration, embodying Hakata's cultural spirit through confectionery.
ai_enrichment.target_audience: Japanese individuals participating in New Year gift-giving and celebratory rituals, alongside international tourists seeking authentic cultural experiences and high-quality traditional Japanese confectionery in Hakata.
ai_enrichment.execution_tactics: In-store experiential activations featuring guided learning and performance of the 'Hakata Te Ippon' ritual upon purchase of special New Year confectionery, complemented by product packaging that explains the ritual's cultural significance.
ai_enrichment.objective: To strengthen Ishimura Manseido's brand as a guardian of Hakata's cultural heritage, foster emotional connection through shared ritual experiences, and boost sales of their New Year confectionery among local and international consumers.

content.description:
In Bolivia, there is a 2.5-liter returnable Coca-Cola bottle—a sustainable and affordable package that you leave at the store and only pay for the beverage inside.

Coca-Cola needed a corporate video about this package that would communicate:That using a returnable Coca-Cola bottle is a conscious and smart choice.That it is produced locally, generating value for domestic consumption during a challenging time for our economy.That it creates jobs for many Bolivians.That there is a highly important production chain behind every returnable Coca-Cola bottle that makes its way to your table.That it is enjoyed from one end of the country to the other.And the most difficult part: everything had to live under the REAL MAGIC platform.

And precisely within that challenge, we found the solution: make the idea magical—transforming a corporate video into a true Coca-Cola spot.Through research, we discovered that no one had ever asked where the Coca-Cola bottle you buy—and return to your neighborhood store when purchasing another one—actually ends up.

So we had our main character ask that very question.

A magical move by a curious young man who wanted to know who would receive the returnable bottle he brought back to the store—and set out to meet that person.

ai_enrichment.concept_summary: The core creative idea is to produce a corporate video that integrates the practical, positive impacts of the 2.5-liter returnable Coca-Cola bottle—such as sustainability, affordability, local job creation, and economic value—with the emotional resonance of the 'REAL MAGIC' brand platform.
ai_enrichment.target_audience: Bolivian consumers who purchase or consider purchasing Coca-Cola's 2.5-liter returnable bottle, particularly those who are conscious of sustainability, local economic impact, and smart purchasing choices.
ai_enrichment.execution_tactics: Production and distribution of a corporate video primarily through digital channels.
ai_enrichment.objective: To educate Bolivian consumers about the economic, social, and environmental benefits of the 2.5-liter returnable Coca-Cola bottle, thereby enhancing brand perception and aligning its value with the 'REAL MAGIC' platform.

metadata.title: Elmo and Mark Rober’s Merry Giftmas
